sections.Narration:
Before the first DAWN touched the land,
Before the SEA had kissed the sand,
The world lay quiet, dark, and DEEP —
The Ancestor Spirits were asleep.
sections.Direction: [ESTABLISH] Wide shot establishing the world. Slow zoom in.
sections.ImagePrompt: Children's book illustration, full page, highly detailed. Scene: The Ancestor Spirits were asleep.. Color palette: rich earth tones. illustrated in a mythological art style. No text or words in the image. Suitable for ages 4-10.
sections.SourceQuote:
— K. Langloh Parker, Australian Legendary Tales (1896) —

The manners and rites of the natives were far the most archaic of all with which we are acquainted. Temples they had none: no images of gods, no altars of sacrifice; scarce any memorials of the dead. Their worship at best was offered in hymns to some vague, half-forgotten deity or First Maker of things, a god decrepit from age or all but careless of his children. Spirits were known and feared, but scarcely defined or described. Sympathetic magic, and perhaps a little hypnotism, were all their science. Kings and nations they knew not; they were wanderers, houseless and homeless. Custom was king; yet custom was tenacious, irresistible, and as complex in minute details as the etiquette of Spanish kings, or the ritual of the Flamens of Rome. The archaic intricacies and taboos of the customs and regulations of marriage might puzzle a mathematician, and may, when unravelled, explain the less complicated prohibitions of a totemism less antique. The people themselves in their struggle for existence had developed great ingenuities.

sections.Wonder: What do you think the world looked like before anyone dreamed it? Can you close your eyes and imagine nothing at all — no light, no sound, no shapes?
sections.Whisper: The Dreaming (Jukurrpa in Warlpiri, Tjukurpa in Pitjantjatjara, Altjeringa in Arrernte) is not a past event — it is the eternal NOW from which all time emerges. Western culture struggles with this: we think of creation as something that happened. In the Dreaming, creation is always happening. The Ancestor Spirits are sleeping AND awake AND walking AND singing simultaneously. This is a 65,000-year-old understanding of non-linear time that Western physics is only now approaching. Listen to Tyson Yunkaporta (Apalech Clan) in 'Sand Talk' for a First Nations perspective on deep time.

sections.Narration:
Then something STIRRED beneath the clay,
A HEARTBEAT pulsed — the Dreaming's way!
The Ancestor Spirits stretched and YAWNED,
And all at once, the world was DAWNED.
sections.Direction: [ESTABLISH] Wide shot establishing the world. Slow zoom in.
sections.ImagePrompt: Children's book illustration, full page, highly detailed. Scene: The Ancestor Spirits stretched and YAWNED,. Color palette: rich earth tones. illustrated in a mythological art style. No text or words in the image. Suitable for ages 4-10.
sections.SourceQuote:
— K. Langloh Parker, Australian Legendary Tales (1896) —

The young men did as they were bade. When they had the bark cut and brought in Wirreenun said: "Go you now and raise with ant-bed a high place, and put thereon logs and wood for a fire, build the ant-bed about a foot from the ground. Then put you a floor of ant-bed a foot high whereever you are going to build a dardurr."

And they did what he told them. When the dardurr were finished, having high floors of ant-bed and water-tight roofs of bark, Wirreenun commanded the whole camp to come with him to the waterhole; men, women, and children; all were to come. They all followed him down to the creek, to the waterhole where he had placed the willgoo willgoo and gubberah. Wirreenun jumped into the water and bade the tribe follow him, which they did. There in the water they all splashed and played about. After a little time Wirreenun went up first behind one black fellow and then behind another, until at length he had been round them all, and taken from the back of each one's head lumps of charcoal. When he went up to each he appeared to suck the back or top of their heads, and to draw out lumps of charcoal, which, as he sucked them out, he spat into the water.

sections.About: Episode 2 of the series. 3 scenes, ~45 seconds total narration.
sections.Arc: Scenes: Then something STIRRED beneath the clay,... → They ROSE up through the ancient ground,... → With EYES that held ten thousand years,...
sections.Wonder: If you were an Ancestor Spirit waking up to make the world, what shape would you take? Would you be an animal, a river, a mountain?
sections.Whisper: In many Aboriginal traditions, the Ancestor Spirits did not create the world from outside — they became the world. A hill IS the body of a resting ancestor. A waterhole IS where an ancestor dug. This is not metaphor. This is ontology — a way of understanding what is real. When Warlpiri people say a rock is an ancestor, they are not being poetic. They are being precise. See Deborah Bird Rose's 'Nourishing Terrains' for deeper understanding of how Country is alive.

sections.Narration:
The Ancestors opened up their THROATS,
And out came SONGS — the sweetest notes!
They SANG the grass, they sang the trees,
They sang the flowers and the BEES.
sections.Direction: [ESTABLISH] Wide shot establishing the world. Slow zoom in.
sections.ImagePrompt: Children's book illustration, full page, highly detailed. Scene: The Ancestors opened up their THROATS,. Color palette: rich earth tones. illustrated in a mythological art style. No text or words in the image. Suitable for ages 4-10.
sections.SourceQuote:
— K. Langloh Parker, Australian Legendary Tales (1896) —

Word had been passed from tribe to tribe, telling, how that the season was good, there must be a great gathering of the tribes. And the place fixed for the gathering was Googoorewon. The old men whispered that it should be the occasion for a borah, but this the women must not know. Old Byamee, who was a great Wirreenun, said he would take his two sons, Ghindahindahmoee and Boomahoomahnowee, to the gathering of the tribes, for the time had come when they should be made young men, that they might be free to marry wives, eat emu flesh, and learn to be warriors.

As tribe after tribe arrived at Googoorewon, each took up a position at one of the various points of the ridges, surrounding the clear open space where the corrobborees were to be. The Wahn, crows, had one point; the Dummerh, pigeons, another; the Mahthi, dogs, another, and so on; Byamee and his tribe, Byahmul the black swans tribe, Oooboon, the blue tongued lizard, and many other chiefs and their tribes, each had their camp on a different point.

sections.Arc: Scenes: The Ancestors opened up their THROATS,... → Each WORD they spoke became a thing —... → They sang in LANGUAGE old and deep,...
sections.Wonder: What if you could sing something into existence? What would your song create?
sections.Whisper: This is the foundational insight of Aboriginal cosmology: the world was SUNG into being. Not spoken (as in Genesis), not thought (as in Hindu Brahman), but sung. Song combines breath, vibration, meaning, and movement — it is creation as embodied act. Bruce Chatwin's 'The Songlines' introduced this concept to Western readers, though many Aboriginal scholars note he oversimplified. Listen to Mandawuy Yunupingu (Yolngu) or read the work of the Strehlow Research Centre for Aboriginal perspectives on song and creation.

sections.Narration:
As Ancestors WALKED from coast to coast,
They left their TRACKS like lines of toast —
Crisscrossing every PLAIN and hill,
Those PATHS are in the landscape still.
sections.Direction: [ESTABLISH] Wide shot establishing the world. Slow zoom in.

sections.SourceQuote:
— K. Langloh Parker, Australian Legendary Tales (1896) —

But that the fish was sun-dried Bootoolgah and Goonur persisted. Day by day passed, and after catching their fish, these two always disappeared, returning with their food looking quite different from that of the others. At last, being unable to extract any information from them, it was determined by the tribe to watch them. Boolooral, the night owl, and Quarrian, the parrot, were appointed to follow the two when they disappeared, to watch where they went, and find out what they did. Accordingly, after the next fish were caught, when Bootoolgah and Goonur gathered up their share and started for the bush, Boolooral and Quarrian followed on their tracks. They saw them disappear into a Bingahwingul scrub, where they lost sight of them. Seeing a high tree on the edge of the scrub, they climbed up it, and from there they saw all that was to be seen. They saw Bootoolgah and Goonur throw down their load of fish, open their comebee and take from it a stick, which stick, when they had blown upon it, they laid in the midst of a heap of leaves and twigs, and at once from this heap they saw a flame leap, which flame the fire makers fed with bigger sticks.

sections.Arc: Scenes: As Ancestors WALKED from coast to coast,... → Each FOOTSTEP was a sacred site,... → A WEB of tracks across the ground,...
sections.Wonder: Do you have a path you walk often — to school, to a friend's house, to the park? What stories does your path tell?
sections.Whisper: Dreaming Tracks (also called Songlines) are the routes Ancestor Spirits walked during the Dreaming. They form a continental network — a 'spaghetti' of paths crossing Australia, connecting sacred sites, water sources, and nations. Different nations are custodians of different sections. To walk a Songline is to re-sing the land into being — maintenance, not just memory. This is simultaneously a navigation system, a legal framework, a spiritual practice, and an ecological knowledge base. Western culture has no single equivalent. See 'Dark Emu' by Bruce Pascoe (Bunurong/Yuin) for how these paths sustained complex civilization.

items.name: Ep5 Scene 1: How Mountains Were Born

sections.Narration:
An ANCESTOR, tired from the track,
Lay down to rest upon their BACK,
Their BODY rose up, strong and steep —
A MOUNTAIN formed while fast asleep!
sections.Direction: [ESTABLISH] Wide shot establishing the world. Slow zoom in.
sections.ImagePrompt: Children's book illustration, full page, highly detailed. Scene: An ANCESTOR, tired from the track,. Color palette: rich earth tones. illustrated in a mythological art style. No text or words in the image. Suitable for ages 4-10.
sections.SourceQuote:
— K. Langloh Parker, Australian Legendary Tales (1896) —

Though I have written my little book in the interests of folk-lore, I hope it will gain the attention of, and have some interest for, children--of Australian children, because they will find stories of old friends among the Bush birds; and of English children, because I hope that they will be glad to make new friends, and so establish a free trade between the Australian and English nurseries--wingless, and laughing birds, in exchange for fairy godmothers, and princes in disguise.

I must also acknowledge my great indebtedness to the blacks, who, when once they understood what I wanted to know, were most ready to repeat to me the legends repeating with the utmost patience, time after time, not only the legends, but the names, that I might manage to spell them so as to be understood when repeated. In particular I should like to mention my indebtedness to Peter Hippi, king of the Noongahburrahs; and to Hippitha, Matah, Barahgurrie, and Beemunny.

I have dedicated my booklet to Peter Hippi, in grateful recognition of his long and faithful service to myself and my husband, which has extended, with few intervals, over a period of twenty years.

sections.Arc: Scenes: An ANCESTOR, tired from the track,... → Another CURLED up on their side,... → So every PEAK you see today,...
sections.Wonder: Next time you see a hill or mountain, can you imagine what shape an ancestor might be sleeping in to make it look like that?
sections.Whisper: This is not just a story — it is the Aboriginal understanding of geology. Uluru (Anangu name) is not 'like' an ancestor; specific features of the rock correspond to specific events in Tjukurpa (the Law/Dreaming of the Anangu people). The caves, waterstains, and marks on Uluru each have narrative meaning maintained by Anangu custodians. Climbing Uluru was closed in 2019 at Anangu request because visitors were walking on a sacred Dreaming path. The rock IS the ancestor. See the Uluru-Kata Tjuta Board of Management's own educational materials for the stories they have chosen to share.

It pushed through ROCK, it pushed through clay,
And where it went — a RIVERWAY!
sections.Direction: [ESTABLISH] Wide shot establishing the world. Slow zoom in.
sections.ImagePrompt: Children's book illustration, full page, highly detailed. Scene: A great SNAKE slithered through the night,. Color palette: rich earth tones. illustrated in a mythological art style. No text or words in the image. Suitable for ages 4-10.
sections.SourceQuote:
— K. Langloh Parker, Australian Legendary Tales (1896) —

The country was stricken with a drought. The rivers were all dry except the deepest holes in them. The grass was dead, and even the trees were dying. The bark dardurr of the blacks were all fallen to the ground and lay there rotting, so long was it since they had been used, for only in wet weather did the blacks use the bark dardurr; at other times they used only whatdooral, or bough shades.

The young men of the Noongahburrah murmured among themselves, at first secretly, at last openly, saying: "Did not our fathers always say that the Wirreenun could make, as we wanted it, the rain to fall? Yet look at our country--the grass blown away, no doonburr seed to grind, the kangaroo are dying, and the emu, the duck, and the swan have flown to far countries. We shall have no food soon; then shall we die, and the Noongahburrah be no more seen on the Narrin. Then why, if he is able, does not Wirreenun inake rain?"

Soon these murmurs reached the ears of the old Wirreenun.

The specificity matters: the Rainbow Serpent is not one being but many related beings with different names, stories, and protocols across different nations. Generalizing them into one 'Rainbow Serpent' is itself a colonial simplification. We use the shared archetype here while acknowledging its diversity. sections.SourceQuote:
— K. Langloh Parker, Australian Legendary Tales (1896) —

Australia makes an appeal to the fancy which is all its own. When Cortes entered Mexico, in the most romantic moment of history, it was as if men had found their way to a new planet, so strange, so long hidden from Europe was all that they beheld. Still they found kings, nobles, peasants, palaces, temples, a great organised society, fauna and flora not so very different from what they had left behind in Spain. In Australia all was novel, and, while seeming fresh, was inestimably old. The vegetation differs from ours; the monotonous grey gum-trees did not resemble our varied forests, but were antique, melancholy, featureless, like their own continent of rare hills, infrequent streams and interminable deserts, concealing nothing within their wastes, yet promising a secret. The birds and beasts--kangaroo, platypus, emu--are ancient types, rough grotesques of Nature, sketching as a child draws. The natives were a race without a history, far more antique than Egypt, nearer the beginnings than any other people. Their weapons are the most primitive: those of the extinct Tasmanians were actually palaeolithic.

sections.Wonder: Have you ever felt like a special place was watching over you — like your backyard or a favorite tree knows who you are?
sections.Whisper: Country (always capitalized in Aboriginal English) is the central concept of Aboriginal ontology. Country is not land — it is a living entity that includes the ground, the sky, the waters, the animals, the plants, the stories, the Law, and the people. Country knows you. Country cares for you if you care for it. Aboriginal people speak of 'listening to Country' as a real practice, not a metaphor. This is animism in its deepest form — not 'everything has a spirit' but 'everything IS spirit, and spirit is relational.' Deborah Bird Rose called it 'an ethic of attention.' See Mary Graham (Kombu-merri/Wakka Wakka) on 'Country as first philosophy.'

sections.Whisper: The Rainbow Serpent is arguably the oldest continuous religious figure in human history — rock art depictions in Arnhem Land date back at least 6,000 years, and the oral tradition far older. Known as Ngalyod to the Kunwinjku people of western Arnhem Land, Borlung to the Miali, Almudj in other Top End languages, and by many other names across the continent. Each nation's serpent is distinct — not one universal being but a family of related Dreaming beings. The sleeping serpent beneath the earth echoes across cultures (Kundalini in Hindu tradition, Jörmungandr in Norse), but this is the oldest version. See Kunwinjku artists at Injalak Arts for their own depictions.

sections.Whisper: The waking of the Rainbow Serpent is an emergence story — creation not from nothing but from within. In Kunwinjku tradition, Ngalyod's movement through the earth shaped the landscape of what is now Kakadu National Park. The geological formations there (sandstone escarpments, floodplains, billabongs) correspond to specific movements of the Serpent. Western geology and Aboriginal Dreaming describe the same landscape features — two knowledge systems reading the same text. Mirrar Elder Yvonne Margarula has spoken publicly about Ngalyod's continuing presence in Kakadu.

sections.Whisper: Waterholes (billabongs) are among the most sacred sites in Aboriginal Australia — many are believed to be places where the Rainbow Serpent rests or entered the ground. Disturbing a waterhole can disturb the Serpent. This is why Aboriginal opposition to mining often centers on water: it's not just environmental concern, it's protecting a living ancestor. The Murray-Darling river system, Australia's largest, carries Dreaming stories along its entire length — different nations custodian different sections. See the Murray Lower Darling Rivers Indigenous Nations (MLDRIN) for how First Nations people manage these waterways today.

sections.SourceQuote:
— K. Langloh Parker, Australian Legendary Tales (1896) —

The manners and rites of the natives were far the most archaic of all with which we are acquainted. Temples they had none: no images of gods, no altars of sacrifice; scarce any memorials of the dead. Their worship at best was offered in hymns to some vague, half-forgotten deity or First Maker of things, a god decrepit from age or all but careless of his children. Spirits were known and feared, but scarcely defined or described. Sympathetic magic, and perhaps a little hypnotism, were all their science. Kings and nations they knew not; they were wanderers, houseless and homeless. Custom was king; yet custom was tenacious, irresistible, and as complex in minute details as the etiquette of Spanish kings, or the ritual of the Flamens of Rome. The archaic intricacies and taboos of the customs and regulations of marriage might puzzle a mathematician, and may, when unravelled, explain the less complicated prohibitions of a totemism less antique. The people themselves in their struggle for existence had developed great ingenuities.

sections.Whisper: The Kimberley region of Western Australia contains some of the oldest rock art on Earth — Gwion Gwion (formerly called 'Bradshaw') figures dating back at least 17,000 years. The Ngarinyin, Worrorra, and Wunambal people are custodians of this art and its associated Dreaming stories. The Wandjina — cloud and rain spirits painted in the Kimberley — are among the most recognized Aboriginal images globally. These are not 'ancient art' in the Western sense — they are repainted by custodians to maintain their spiritual power. The art is alive. See the Mowanjum Art Centre (Derby, WA) for Wandjina art shared by its custodians.

sections.SourceQuote:
— K. Langloh Parker, Australian Legendary Tales (1896) —

16. MULLYANGAH THE MORNING STAR

Mullyan, the eagle hawk, built himself a home high in a yaraan tree. There he lived apart from his tribe, with Moodai the opossum, his wife, and Moodai the opossum, his mother-in-law. With them too was Buttergah, a daughter of the Buggoo or flying squirrel tribe. Buttergah was a friend of Moodai, the wife of Mullyan, and a distant cousin to the Moodai tribe.

Mullyan the eagle hawk was a cannibal. That was the reason of his living apart from the other blacks. In order to satisfy his cannibal cravings, he used to sally forth with a big spear, a spear about four times as big as an ordinary spear. If he found a black fellow hunting alone, he would kill him and take his body up to the house in the tree. There the Moodai and Buttergab would cook it, and all of them would eat the flesh; for the women as well as Mullyan were cannibals. This went on for some time, until at last so many black fellows were slain that their friends determined to find out what became of them, and they tracked the last one they missed.

sections.Whisper: Aboriginal Law (always capitalized — it is constitutional, not advisory) is understood to come directly from the Dreaming. It governs land management, kinship, ceremony, resource sharing, and conflict resolution. It is not 'belief' — it is law, with consequences for violation. The Rainbow Serpent is often the enforcer: floods, storms, and droughts can be understood as the Serpent responding to broken Law. This legal system has operated continuously for tens of thousands of years — far longer than any Western legal tradition. See the work of Irene Watson (Tanganekald/Meintangk) on Aboriginal law as living legal system.

metadata.tradition: Aboriginal Law traditions (publicly discussed by Aboriginal legal scholars)

— K. Langloh Parker, Australian Legendary Tales (1896) —

I have dedicated my booklet to Peter Hippi, in grateful recognition of his long and faithful service to myself and my husband, which has extended, with few intervals, over a period of twenty years. He, too, is probably the last king of the Noongabburrahs, who are fast dying out--, and soon their weapons, bartered by them for tobacco or whisky, alone will prove that they ever existed. It seemed to me a pity that some attempt should not be made to collect the folk-lore of the quickly disappearing tribe--a folk-lore embodying, probably, the thoughts, fancies, and beliefs of the genuine aboriginal race, and which, as such, deserves to be, indeed, as Max Muller says, "might be and ought to be, collected in every part of the world."

The legends were told to me by the blacks themselves, some of whom remember the coming of Mitchellan, as they call Major Mitchell, the explorer of these back creeks. The old blacks laugh now when they tell you how frightened their mothers were of the first wheel tracks they saw.

sections.Whisper: Flood stories associated with the Rainbow Serpent appear across Aboriginal Australia — and they may encode actual geological events. Rising sea levels at the end of the last Ice Age (roughly 10,000-7,000 years ago) flooded Australia's continental shelf, and Aboriginal oral traditions appear to accurately describe coastlines that haven't existed for millennia. This is potentially the longest-maintained accurate oral history on Earth. Nick Reid and Patrick Nunn's research on Aboriginal sea-level narratives shows 21 communities accurately describing post-glacial flooding. The stories are not 'myths' — they are data.

sections.SourceQuote:
— K. Langloh Parker, Australian Legendary Tales (1896) —

"Go, women, stay not to talk. Did ye ever see talk make a fire burn? Then why stand ye there talking? Go; do as I bid you. Talk not so foolishly; if you ran away soon should I catch you, and, catching you, would beat you hard. Go I talk no more."

The Meamei went, taking with them their combos with which to cut the bark. They went each to a different tree, and each, with a strong hit, drove her combo into the bark. As she did so, each felt the tree that her combo had struck rising higher out of the ground and bearing her upward with it. Higher and higher grew the pine trees, and still on them, higher and higher from the earth, went the two girls. Hearing no chopping after the first hits, Wurrunnah came towards the pines to see what was keeping the girls so long. As he came near them he saw that the pine trees were growing taller even as he looked at them, and clinging to the trunks of the trees high in the air he saw his two wives. He called to them to come down, but they made no answer. Time after time he called to them as higher and higher they went, but still they made no answer. Steadily taller grew the two pines, until at last their tops touched the sky.

sections.Whisper: The association of the Rainbow Serpent with actual rainbows is a visible, daily connection between the Dreaming and the present. It is not that the rainbow 'represents' the Serpent — in many traditions, the rainbow IS the Serpent, visible in the sky. This collapse of symbol and reality is fundamental to Aboriginal ontology. A rainbow after rain is the Serpent moving between waterholes through the sky. Anthropologist Howard Morphy (working with Yolngu permission) has written extensively about how Yolngu art encodes Dreaming knowledge in patterns visible to initiated eyes. The rainbow is perhaps the most publicly visible Dreaming event.

Though I have written my little book in the interests of folk-lore, I hope it will gain the attention of, and have some interest for, children--of Australian children, because they will find stories of old friends among the Bush birds; and of English children, because I hope that they will be glad to make new friends, and so establish a free trade between the Australian and English nurseries--wingless, and laughing birds, in exchange for fairy godmothers, and princes in disguise.

sections.Whisper: Aboriginal astronomy is one of the most sophisticated ancient astronomical traditions on Earth. While Western astronomy focuses on stars (bright points), Aboriginal astronomy equally maps the dark nebulae — the dark spaces between stars. The 'Emu in the Sky' is formed by the Coalsack Nebula (the head) extending along dark dust lanes of the Milky Way. Its orientation changes seasonally, and when the Emu 'sits down' (legs tucked), it signals emu egg-laying season. This is practical, precise astronomical knowledge embedded in story. See Duane Hamacher's 'The First Astronomers' (written with Ghillar Michael Anderson, Euahlayi nation) for Aboriginal astronomical knowledge systems.

metadata.tradition: Aboriginal dark-sky astronomy (multiple nations, publicly shared — notably Kamilaroi/Euahlayi)

— K. Langloh Parker, Australian Legendary Tales (1896) —

The next day Wurrunnah made a fresh start, and left the camp of the Meamei, as if he were leaving for good. But he determined to hide near and watch what they did, and if he could get a chance he would steal a wife from amongst them. He was tired of travelling alone. He saw the seven sisters all start out with their yam sticks in hand. He followed at a distance, taking care not to be seen. He saw them stop by the nests of some flying ants. With their yam sticks they dug all round these ant holes. When they had successfully unearthed the ants they sat down, throwing their yam sticks on one side, to enjoy a feast, for these ants were esteemed by them a great delicacy.

While the sisters were busy at their feast, Wurrunnah sneaked up to their yam sticks and stole two of them; then, taking the sticks with him, sneaked back to his hiding-place. When at length the Meamei had satisfied their appetites, they picked up their sticks and turned towards their camp again. But only five could find their sticks; so those five started off, leaving the other two to find theirs, supposing they must be somewhere near, and, finding them, they would soon catch them up.

sections.Wonder: The Seven Sisters stayed together because they were stronger as a group. Who are the people you'd want in your cluster of stars?
sections.Whisper: The Pleiades (Seven Sisters) story exists in Aboriginal, Greek, Norse, Japanese, and many other traditions — but the Aboriginal version may be the oldest. Recent research by Duane Hamacher and Ray Norris suggests the story may encode the observation that the Pleiades once had a visible seventh star (Pleione) that faded ~100,000 years ago. If Aboriginal oral tradition preserves this astronomical observation, it is the longest-maintained scientific record in human history. The pursuing hunter is often identified with Orion. In many Aboriginal versions, the sisters are the Kungkarungkara (Seven Sisters) of the Western Desert, and their story is a Tjukurpa that crosses multiple nations' Country.

sections.SourceQuote:
— K. Langloh Parker, Australian Legendary Tales (1896) —

Australia makes an appeal to the fancy which is all its own. When Cortes entered Mexico, in the most romantic moment of history, it was as if men had found their way to a new planet, so strange, so long hidden from Europe was all that they beheld. Still they found kings, nobles, peasants, palaces, temples, a great organised society, fauna and flora not so very different from what they had left behind in Spain. In Australia all was novel, and, while seeming fresh, was inestimably old. The vegetation differs from ours; the monotonous grey gum-trees did not resemble our varied forests, but were antique, melancholy, featureless, like their own continent of rare hills, infrequent streams and interminable deserts, concealing nothing within their wastes, yet promising a secret. The birds and beasts--kangaroo, platypus, emu--are ancient types, rough grotesques of Nature, sketching as a child draws. The natives were a race without a history, far more antique than Egypt, nearer the beginnings than any other people. Their weapons are the most primitive: those of the extinct Tasmanians were actually palaeolithic.

sections.Arc: Scenes: Five STARS arranged like precious gems,... → Or maybe it's an EAGLE'S claw,... → The POINTER stars show where to look,...
sections.Wonder: Different people see different things in the same stars — an eagle, a stingray, a cross. What shape do YOU see in five stars arranged like a kite?
sections.Whisper: The Southern Cross (Crux) has different Dreaming stories across Aboriginal nations — it is not one story but many. To the Boorong people of western Victoria, the cross stars are Bunya (the ring-tailed possum) being pursued. To others, it is a stingray, or the footprint of the wedge-tailed eagle Waluwara. The two Pointer stars (Alpha and Beta Centauri) are often two brothers or two hunters. This multiplicity of readings is not confusion — it is a feature. Each nation reads its own Country into the sky. The same stars carry different Law for different peoples. See John Morieson's work with Boorong astronomical knowledge.

metadata.tradition: Multiple nations' readings of Crux (Boorong, publicly documented by Stanbridge 1857)

sections.SourceQuote:
— K. Langloh Parker, Australian Legendary Tales (1896) —

The black fellows said they wanted wives, and would each take one, and both care for the children. The women agreed. The black fellows swam back across the river, each taking a child first, and then a woman, for as they came from the back country, where no creeks were, the women could not swim.

Goomblegubbon came back from hunting, and, seeing no wives, called aloud for them, but heard no answer. Then he went to their camp, and found them not. Then turning towards the dungle he saw that it was empty. Then he saw the tracks of his wives and children going towards the river. Great was his anger, and vowing he would kill them when he found them, he picked up his spears and followed their tracks, until he too reached the river. There on the other side he saw a camp, and in it he could see strange black fellows, his wives, and his children. He called aloud for them to cross him over, for he too could not swim. But the sun went down and still they did not answer.

sections.About: Episode 18 of the series. 3 scenes, ~43 seconds total narration.
sections.Arc: Scenes: The SUN is not a ball of fire,... → She paints her FACE with red and gold —... → At DUSK she paints herself once more,...
sections.Wonder: In this story, the Sun is a woman who gets up every single morning and does her job. What job do you do every day, no matter what?
sections.Whisper: In many Aboriginal traditions, the Sun is female — Walu to Yolngu people, Gnowee to the Wurundjeri of the Kulin nation. This contrasts with most Indo-European traditions where the sun is male. The Sun Woman's daily journey — painting herself with ochre at dawn and dusk (explaining the red sky), walking across the sky, and traveling underground at night — is a complete astronomical model wrapped in story. The red ochre reference connects sky phenomena to earth materials to ceremonial body-painting to women's business. Everything is connected. See Diane Johnson and Duane Hamacher's work on Aboriginal solar traditions.

metadata.tradition: Yolngu and Wurundjeri sun traditions (publicly shared)

sections.SourceQuote:
— K. Langloh Parker, Australian Legendary Tales (1896) —

An old man lived with his two wives, the Mooninguggahgul sisters, and his two sons. The old man spent all his time making boomerangs, until at last he had four nets full of these weapons. The two boys used to go out hunting opossums and iguanas, which they would cook in the bush, and eat, without thinking of bringing any home to their parents. The old man asked them one day to bring him home some fat to rub his boomerangs with. This the boys did, but they brought only the fat, having eaten the rest of the iguanas from which they had taken the fat. The old man was very angry that his sons were so greedy, but he said nothing, though he determined to punish them, for he thought "when they were young, and could not hunt, I hunted for them and fed them well; now that they can hunt and I am old and cannot so well, they give me nothing." Thinking of his treatment at the hands of his sons, he greased all his boomerangs, and when he had finished them he said to the boys: "You take these boomerangs down on to the plain and try them; see if I have made them well. Then come back and tell me. I will stay here."

The boys took the boomerangs.

sections.Arc: Scenes: The MOON is an old man, thin and round,... → He gets so FAT and full and bright,... → He DIES each month but comes back new,...
sections.Wonder: The Moon gets thin, then fat, then thin again — always changing but always coming back. What in your life changes but always comes back?
sections.Whisper: In many Aboriginal traditions, the Moon is male (complementing the female Sun). The Yolngu people know him as Ngalindi — a fat, lazy man who was punished by his wives for not sharing food, and who wastes away each month before being reborn. The lunar cycle as death-and-rebirth is one of humanity's oldest metaphors, and Aboriginal versions are among the most ancient. The Moon's phases were used to time ceremonies, manage resources, and mark women's cycles. Yolngu astronomer and educator Duane Hamacher (working with Yolngu elders) has documented how lunar observations encode practical knowledge about tides, fishing, and seasonal timing.

metadata.tradition: Yolngu lunar tradition — Ngalindi (publicly shared through educational programs)

sections.Narration:

sections.Direction: [ESTABLISH] Wide shot establishing the world. Slow zoom in.

sections.SourceQuote:
— K. Langloh Parker, Australian Legendary Tales (1896) —

The manners and rites of the natives were far the most archaic of all with which we are acquainted. Temples they had none: no images of gods, no altars of sacrifice; scarce any memorials of the dead. Their worship at best was offered in hymns to some vague, half-forgotten deity or First Maker of things, a god decrepit from age or all but careless of his children. Spirits were known and feared, but scarcely defined or described. Sympathetic magic, and perhaps a little hypnotism, were all their science. Kings and nations they knew not; they were wanderers, houseless and homeless. Custom was king; yet custom was tenacious, irresistible, and as complex in minute details as the etiquette of Spanish kings, or the ritual of the Flamens of Rome. The archaic intricacies and taboos of the customs and regulations of marriage might puzzle a mathematician, and may, when unravelled, explain the less complicated prohibitions of a totemism less antique. The people themselves in their struggle for existence had developed great ingenuities.

items.name: Episode 20: The River in the Sky

sections.About: Episode 20 of the series. 3 scenes, ~39 seconds total narration.
sections.Arc: Scenes: Look UP and see the river flow,... → Some nations say it's CAMPFIRE smoke,... → Others say it's the great SKY RIVER,...
sections.Wonder: The Milky Way could be a river, or campfire smoke, or a road. Lie on your back and look up — what does it look like to you?
sections.Whisper: The Milky Way has perhaps more Aboriginal names and stories than any other sky feature — precisely because it dominates the southern sky. To some nations, it is a river (Birrarung — the Wurundjeri name that also names the Yarra River, connecting sky-river to earth-river). To others, it is the smoke of celestial campfires. The dark patches within it form the Emu. The bright patches contain the Seven Sisters. It is a road for spirits, a seasonal calendar, and a story-map. The fact that different nations see it differently — and that all versions are 'true' — demonstrates the Aboriginal principle that Country speaks differently to different custodians. See 'Emu Dreaming' by Ray Norris and Cilla Norris.

metadata.tradition: Multiple nations' Milky Way traditions (Wurundjeri, Yolngu, Kamilaroi, publicly shared)

GLOSSARY

Bahloo, moon. Beeargah, hawk. Beeleer, black cockatoo. Beereeun, prickly lizard. Bibbee, woodpecker, bird. Bibbil, shiny-leaved box-tree. Bilber, a large kind of rat. Billai or Billay, crimson-wing parrot. Bindeah, a prickle or small thorn. Bingah wingul, needle bush, a tall thorny shrub. Birrahgnooloo, woman's name, meaning "face like a tomahawk handle." Birrahlee, baby. Birrableegul, children. Boobootella, the big bunch of feathers at the back of an emu. Boolooral, an owl. Boomerang, a curved weapon used in hunting and in warfare by the blacks; called Burren by the Narran blacks. Bootoolgah, blue-grey crane. Borah, a large gathering of blacks where the boys are initiated into the mysteries which make them young men. Bou-gou-doo-gahdah, the rain bird. Like the bower or mocking bird. Bouyou, legs. Bowrah or Bohrah, kangaroo. Bralgahs, native companion, bird. Bubberah, boomerang that returns. Buckandee, native cat. Buggoo, flying squirrel. Bulgahnunnoo, bark-backed. Bumble, a fruit-bearing tree, sometimes called wild orange and sometimes wild pomegranate tree. Capparis. Bunbundoolooey, brown flock pigeon. Bunnyyarl, flies.

sections.Wonder: The Morning Star is a message from people who love us. If you could send a star-message to someone, who would you send it to? What would it say?
sections.Whisper: The Morning Star ceremony (Banumbirr) is one of the most important Yolngu ceremonies — and one of the few that has been publicly shared and performed for non-Aboriginal audiences, most famously at the opening of the new Parliament House in 1988. The Morning Star (Venus) is understood as a spirit sent on a long string from the island of the dead (Burralku) back to the living. The string can be pulled back, bringing the star home. This is simultaneously astronomy, grief ritual, theology, and performance art. The Bangarra Dance Theatre (founded by Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander artists) has performed versions of the Morning Star ceremony on world stages.

Old Byamee said to his two young wives, Birrahgnooloo and Cunnunbeillee, "I have stuck a white feather between the hind legs of a bee, and am going to let it go and then follow it to its nest, that I may get honey. While I go for the honey, go you two out and get frogs and yams, then meet me at Coorigel Spring, where we will camp, for sweet and clear is the water there." The wives, taking their goolays and yam sticks, went out as he told them. Having gone far, and dug out many yams and frogs, they were tired when they reached Coorigel, and, seeing the cool, fresh water, they longed to bathe. But first they built a bough shade, and there left their goolays holding their food, and the yams and frogs they had found. When their camp was ready for the coming of Byamee, who having wooed his wives with a nullah-nullah, kept them obedient by fear of the same weapon, then went the girls to the spring to bathe. Gladly they plunged in, having first divested them selves of their goomillahs, which they were still young enough to wear, and which they left on the ground near the spring.

sections.Whisper: Tiddalick is one of the most widely shared Aboriginal stories — told across southeastern Australia by Gunai/Kurnai and other nations. It is often the first Aboriginal story taught in Australian schools, and Aboriginal educators have actively shared it for cross-cultural understanding. The story teaches resource sharing (don't hoard water) and community problem-solving (the animals worked together). Nabunum the eel's solution — making the hoarder laugh — is conflict resolution through humor rather than force. This is a deeply Aboriginal approach: problems are solved through relationship, not coercion. See the Koorie Heritage Trust (Melbourne) for Aboriginal educational resources.

sections.Whisper: The kookaburra wake-up call story is shared by several nations in eastern Australia, including the Wiradjuri. The laughing kookaburra (Dacelo novaeguineae) is endemic to Australia and its distinctive call is one of the continent's signature sounds. In the story, the kookaburra was given a sacred duty — to announce the dawn so that humans would not miss the gift of sunlight. Some versions say that if kookaburra ever stops laughing, the sun will not rise. This transforms an everyday bird call into a daily miracle. Note: the well-known children's song 'Kookaburra Sits in the Old Gum Tree' (1932) was written by non-Aboriginal teacher Marion Sinclair and is not an Aboriginal song, though it draws on the same love of this bird.

sections.Whisper: The Crow and Eagle fire story is told by the Kulin nations of central Victoria — Wurundjeri, Bunurong, Boon Wurrung, and others. Crow (Waa) and Eagle (Bunjil) are moiety ancestors — the two halves into which all Kulin people are divided. Every person is either Waa or Bunjil, which determines marriage rules, ceremonial roles, and relationships with Country. This story is not just about fire — it encodes the fundamental social structure of Kulin society. Bunjil the Eagle is also the creator spirit of the Kulin — the great ancestor who made the land, the people, and the Law. See the Wurundjeri Woi-wurrung Cultural Heritage Aboriginal Corporation for Bunjil's story as told by his custodians.

sections.Wonder: Platypus belongs to lots of groups but doesn't fit perfectly in any one. Do you ever feel like you belong to many different groups at once?
sections.Whisper: The platypus classification story appears in several Aboriginal traditions, often as a story about what happens when animal groups fight and a child is born between them. The platypus — which genuinely confounded European taxonomy (scientists initially thought the first specimen was a hoax) — makes perfect sense in Aboriginal ontology where beings can be multiple things simultaneously. The story teaches that rigid categories are a problem of the classifier, not the classified. This resonates with Aboriginal kinship systems, which are far more complex and flexible than Western nuclear-family models. The platypus is Country's reminder that life refuses boxes. See Aboriginal educator Aunty Joy Murphy (Wurundjeri) on how Aboriginal stories teach flexible thinking.

sections.Whisper: Wombat stories are shared by several southeastern nations. The wombat's burrow-digging is not just metaphor — recent ecological research has shown that wombat burrows genuinely serve as refuge for other animals during bushfires and floods. After the catastrophic 2019-2020 Australian bushfires, wombat burrows sheltered small mammals and reptiles. Aboriginal people knew this for millennia. The wombat as generous shelter-builder aligns with the Aboriginal principle that every being has a role in maintaining Country's health. This is ecology as ethics, responsibility as identity. See the work of Aboriginal fire ecologists like Victor Steffensen (Tagalaka) in 'Fire Country' for how Aboriginal knowledge and Western ecology are converging.

sections.SourceQuote:
— K. Langloh Parker, Australian Legendary Tales (1896) —

All the tribes agreed to come, and soon all were engaged in great preparations. Each determined to outdo the other in the quaintness and brightness of their painting for the corrobboree. Each tribe as they arrived gained great applause; never before had the young people seen so much diversity in colouring and design. Beeleer, the Black Cockatoo tribe, came with bright splashes of orange-red on their black skins. The Pelicans came as a contrast, almost pure white, only a touch here and there of their black skin showing where the white paint had rubbed off. The Black Divers came in their black skins, but these polished to shine like satin. Then came the Millears, the beauties of the Kangaroo Rat family, who had their home on the morillas. After them came the Buckandeer or Native Cat tribe, painted in dull colours, but in all sorts of patterns. Mairas or Paddymelons came too in haste to take part in the great corrobboree. After them, walking slowly, came the Bralgahs, looking tall and dignified as they held up their red heads, painted so in contrast to their French-grey bodies, which they deemed too dull a colour, unbrightened, for such a gay occasion.

sections.Whisper: This story, shared by several nations, is an etiological tale — a 'why' story explaining an animal feature through moral action. The kangaroo's pouch as reward for compassion teaches that physical characteristics are not random but meaningful: the body itself is a record of moral history. This connects to the broader Aboriginal understanding that landscape, body, and story are one continuous text. The kangaroo (particularly the red kangaroo, Macropus rufus) is central to many nations' Dreamings and is a key food and spiritual resource. The manners and rites of the natives were far the most archaic of all with which we are acquainted. Temples they had none: no images of gods, no altars of sacrifice; scarce any memorials of the dead. Their worship at best was offered in hymns to some vague, half-forgotten deity or First Maker of things, a god decrepit from age or all but careless of his children. Spirits were known and feared, but scarcely defined or described. Sympathetic magic, and perhaps a little hypnotism, were all their science. Custom was king; yet custom was tenacious, irresistible, and as complex in minute details as the etiquette of Spanish kings, or the ritual of the Flamens of Rome. The archaic intricacies and taboos of the customs and regulations of marriage might puzzle a mathematician, and may, when unravelled, explain the less complicated prohibitions of a totemism less antique. sections.Whisper: Fire stories are among the most important in Aboriginal tradition because Aboriginal Australians are the world's oldest and most sophisticated fire managers. Aboriginal fire-stick farming — burning small patches of Country in careful rotation — maintained Australia's ecology for tens of thousands of years. When colonization stopped these burns, catastrophic bushfires became more common. The 2019-2020 fires led to renewed interest in Aboriginal burning practices. The goanna's flickering tongue 'remembering' fire is a beautiful image of knowledge stored in the body. Victor Steffensen's 'Fire Country' and cultural burning programs across Australia are restoring this ancient practice. See also the work of the Firesticks Alliance, an Aboriginal-led network.

The next day a shift was made of the camps. They were moved to inside the big ring that the black fellows had made. This move was attended with a certain amount of ceremony. In the afternoon, before the move had taken place, all the black fellows left their camps and went away into the scrub. Every man had a fire stick in one hand and a green switch in the other. sections.Whisper: Walking Country is not hiking. It is a spiritual, legal, and ecological practice. When Aboriginal people walk their Country, they are simultaneously: navigating using Dreaming knowledge, maintaining their legal responsibility to that land, checking ecological health, performing ceremony through the act of walking itself, and re-singing the Songlines that keep the land alive. To not walk Country is to let it suffer. This is custodianship as embodied practice — you maintain the world by moving through it with attention. See Tyson Yunkaporta's 'Sand Talk' for how walking-as-thinking operates in Aboriginal knowledge systems.

sections.Whisper: Songlines (also called Dreaming Tracks) are perhaps the most remarkable knowledge system ever devised. They encode navigation, law, ecology, and spirituality into musical sequences that can be transmitted across language barriers — because the melodic contour maps the terrain's contour. A song's rhythm literally mirrors the landscape it describes. Different nations custodian different sections of a Songline, and when they meet at ceremony, they sing their sections in sequence, creating a continental map in song. Bruce Chatwin's 'The Songlines' (1987) popularized the concept but oversimplified it. For Aboriginal perspectives, see the work of the Anangu Pitjantjatjara Yankunytjatjara (APY) Lands council.

sections.SourceQuote:
— K. Langloh Parker, Australian Legendary Tales (1896) —

Word had been passed from tribe to tribe, telling, how that the season was good, there must be a great gathering of the tribes. And the place fixed for the gathering was Googoorewon. The old men whispered that it should be the occasion for a borah, but this the women must not know. Old Byamee, who was a great Wirreenun, said he would take his two sons, Ghindahindahmoee and Boomahoomahnowee, to the gathering of the tribes, for the time had come when they should be made young men, that they might be free to marry wives, eat emu flesh, and learn to be warriors.

As tribe after tribe arrived at Googoorewon, each took up a position at one of the various points of the ridges, surrounding the clear open space where the corrobborees were to be. The Wahn, crows, had one point; the Dummerh, pigeons, another; the Mahthi, dogs, another, and so on; Byamee and his tribe, Byahmul the black swans tribe, Oooboon, the blue tongued lizard, and many other chiefs and their tribes, each had their camp on a different point.

sections.Whisper: The concept of landscape-as-music is not metaphor in Aboriginal understanding. Research by musicologist Linda Barwick (working with Warlpiri and Murrinh-Patha communities) has shown that Songline melodies physically mirror terrain profiles — the pitch rises when the track goes uphill, falls when it descends, and rhythm changes mirror changes in terrain texture. This means the songs contain actual topographic data encoded in music. A person who knows the song can navigate Country they have never visited, because the song IS the map. This is arguably the most sophisticated mnemonic system ever created — encoding continental-scale geographic data in singable form. See 'Singing the Land, Signing the Land' by anthropologists.

6. THE WEEOONIBEENS AND THE PIGGIEBILLAH

Two Weeoombeen brothers went out hunting. One brother was much younger than the other and smaller, so when they sighted an emu, the elder one said to the younger: "You stay quietly here and do not make a noise, or Piggiebillah, whose camp we passed just now, will hear you and steal the emu if I kill it. He is so strong. I'll go on and try to kill the emu with this stone." The little Weeoombeen watched his big brother sneak up to the emu, crawling along, almost flat, on the ground. He saw him get quite close to the emu, then spring up quickly and throw the stone with such an accurate aim as to kill the bird on the spot. The little brother was so rejoiced that he forgot his brother's caution, and he called aloud in his joy. The big Weeoombeen looked round and gave him a warning sign, but too late, Piggiebillah had heard the cry and was hastening towards them.

She describes it as 'the deep spring that is inside us... a form of contemplation.' Dadirri is not meditation imported from elsewhere — it is an Aboriginal practice of listening to Country that has been maintained for millennia. Ungunmerr-Baumann has explicitly shared this concept with non-Aboriginal audiences as a gift. When Country 'speaks,' the listener must be in a state of Dadirri to hear. sections.SourceQuote:
— K. Langloh Parker, Australian Legendary Tales (1896) —

The legends were told to me by the blacks themselves, some of whom remember the coming of Mitchellan, as they call Major Mitchell, the explorer of these back creeks. The old blacks laugh now when they tell you how frightened their mothers were of the first wheel tracks they saw. They would not let the children tread on them, but carefully lifted them over, lest their feet should break out in sores, as they were supposed to do if they trod on a snake's track. But with all their fear, little did they realise that the coming of Mitchellan was the beginning of their end, or that fifty years afterwards, from the remnant of their once numerous tribe, would be collected the legends they told in those days to their piccaninnies round their camp-fires, and those legends used to make a Christmas booklet for the children of their white supplanters.

I can only hope that the white children will be as ready to listen to these stories as were, and indeed are, the little piccaninnies, and thus the sale of this booklet be such as to enable me to add frocks and tobacco when I give their Christmas dinner, as is my yearly custom, to the remnant of the Noongahburrahs.

K.

You don't just live ON Country; Country lives IN you, through generations of relationship. This is not genetic memory in the Western scientific sense but relational memory: the land knows your lineage because your lineage has tended it. When Aboriginal people are displaced from Country (as happened systematically through colonization and the Stolen Generations), the loss is not just of 'home' but of a reciprocal relationship maintained for millennia. See the Healing Foundation's work on intergenerational trauma and Country connection.

sections.Narration:

sections.SourceQuote:
— K. Langloh Parker, Australian Legendary Tales (1896) —

One day, Googarh, the iguana, and his two wives went out hunting, leaving the two little Cookooburrahs at the camp. They had taken out water for themselves in their opossum skin water bags, but they had left none for the children, who were too small to get any from the goolahgool for themselves, so nearly perished from thirst. Their tongues were swollen in their mouths, and they were quite speechless, when they saw a man coming towards them. When he came near, they saw it was Cookooburrah, their big brother. They could not speak to him and answer, when he asked where his mother was. Then he asked them what was the matter. All they could do was to point towards the tree. He looked at it, and saw it was a goolahgool, so he said: "Did your mother leave you no water?" They shook their heads. He said: "Then you are perishing for want of a drink, my brothers?" They nodded. "Go," he said "a little way off, and you shall see how I will punish them for leaving my little brothers to perish of thirst." He went towards the tree, climbed up it, and split it right down. As he did so, out gushed the water in a swiftly running stream.

sections.Wonder: Aboriginal people found water in the desert by singing special songs. If you had to hide something important, would you hide it in a song? What song?
sections.Whisper: Aboriginal water-finding through Songline knowledge is one of the most practically remarkable aspects of the Dreaming. In the Western Desert, where surface water is scarce, Songlines encode the locations of underground water sources (soakages, native wells) that are invisible to the uninitiated eye. The songs describe landmarks, distances, and digging techniques with precision sufficient to sustain life in one of Earth's harshest environments. This knowledge system sustained Aboriginal civilization in the Australian interior for at least 50,000 years. When this knowledge was disrupted by colonization, people died of thirst in country their ancestors had navigated for millennia. See 'The Last of the Nomads' by W.J. Peasley for the story of Warri and Yatungka, the last people living traditionally in the Gibson Desert.

metadata.tradition: Western Desert water-finding traditions (publicly documented with Aboriginal collaboration)

sections.SourceQuote:
— K. Langloh Parker, Australian Legendary Tales (1896) —

The manners and rites of the natives were far the most archaic of all with which we are acquainted. Temples they had none: no images of gods, no altars of sacrifice; scarce any memorials of the dead. Their worship at best was offered in hymns to some vague, half-forgotten deity or First Maker of things, a god decrepit from age or all but careless of his children. Spirits were known and feared, but scarcely defined or described. Sympathetic magic, and perhaps a little hypnotism, were all their science. Kings and nations they knew not; they were wanderers, houseless and homeless. Custom was king; yet custom was tenacious, irresistible, and as complex in minute details as the etiquette of Spanish kings, or the ritual of the Flamens of Rome. The archaic intricacies and taboos of the customs and regulations of marriage might puzzle a mathematician, and may, when unravelled, explain the less complicated prohibitions of a totemism less antique. The people themselves in their struggle for existence had developed great ingenuities.

sections.Wonder: Where is your meeting place — the spot where your friends all come together? What makes a place feel like it belongs to everyone?
sections.Whisper: Songline intersections were (and are) sites for major inter-nation gatherings — corroborees, trade fairs, and ceremony. The best-documented is the gathering at Lake Mungo (Willandra Lakes), where up to 500 people from multiple nations would meet. Melbourne's CBD sits on a major meeting place of the Kulin nations, where the Yarra River (Birrarung) intersections drew people from the Wurundjeri, Bunurong, Boon Wurrung, Taungurung, and Dja Dja Wurrung. These were not just social events but legal and diplomatic assemblies — disputes were settled, marriages arranged, knowledge exchanged, and ceremonies performed. The concept of 'terra nullius' (empty land) that justified colonization ignored a continent-wide civilization organized along these meeting networks. See Bruce Pascoe's 'Dark Emu' for evidence of complex Aboriginal social infrastructure.

sections.SourceQuote:
— K. Langloh Parker, Australian Legendary Tales (1896) —

Though I have written my little book in the interests of folk-lore, I hope it will gain the attention of, and have some interest for, children--of Australian children, because they will find stories of old friends among the Bush birds; and of English children, because I hope that they will be glad to make new friends, and so establish a free trade between the Australian and English nurseries--wingless, and laughing birds, in exchange for fairy godmothers, and princes in disguise.

I must also acknowledge my great indebtedness to the blacks, who, when once they understood what I wanted to know, were most ready to repeat to me the legends repeating with the utmost patience, time after time, not only the legends, but the names, that I might manage to spell them so as to be understood when repeated. In particular I should like to mention my indebtedness to Peter Hippi, king of the Noongahburrahs; and to Hippitha, Matah, Barahgurrie, and Beemunny.

I have dedicated my booklet to Peter Hippi, in grateful recognition of his long and faithful service to myself and my husband, which has extended, with few intervals, over a period of twenty years.

sections.Wonder: Who are the elders in your life — grandparents, teachers, neighbors? What have they taught you that you couldn't learn from a book?
sections.Whisper: Aboriginal knowledge transmission is the longest continuous educational tradition on Earth. Knowledge is passed orally, through ceremony, through art, through walking Country — never (traditionally) through writing. This is not because Aboriginal people lacked the capacity for writing, but because oral transmission carries dimensions that text cannot: tone, gesture, context, relationship. You cannot learn sacred knowledge from a book because the relationship between teacher and learner IS part of the knowledge. Elder authority is not about age alone but about demonstrated responsibility: you earn the right to teach by showing you can hold knowledge without misusing it. See Uncle Jack Charles's autobiography and the work of Aunty Joy Murphy Wandin (Wurundjeri Elder) on intergenerational knowledge.

metadata.tradition: Pan-Aboriginal oral knowledge transmission (publicly discussed by many Aboriginal educators)

sections.SourceQuote:
— K. Langloh Parker, Australian Legendary Tales (1896) —

These, for the most part, are KINDER MARCHEN, though they include many aetiological myths, explanatory of the markings and habits of animals, the origin of constellations, and so forth. They are a savage edition of the METAMORPHOSES, and few unbiased students now doubt that the METAMORPHOSES are a very late and very artificial version of traditional tales as savage in origin as those of the Noongahburrah. I have read Mrs. Parker's collection with very great interest, with "human pleasure," merely for the story's sake. Children will find here the Jungle Book, never before printed, of black little boys and girls. The sympathy with, and knowledge of beast-life and bird-life are worthy of Mr. Kipling, and the grotesque names are just what children like. Dinewan and Goomblegubbon should take their place with Rikki Tikki and Mr. Kipling's other delightful creatures. But there is here no Mowgli, set apart in the jungle as a man. Man, bird, and beast are all blended in the Australian fancy as in that of Bushmen and Red Indians. All are of one kindred, all shade into each other; all obey the Bush Law as they obey the Jungle Law in Mr. Kipling's fascinating stories.

sections.Wonder: Before you pick a flower or catch a bug, do you ever stop and ask if it's okay? What do you think nature would say?
sections.Whisper: Listening to Country is the foundational environmental ethic of Aboriginal Australia — and it is not metaphor. Aboriginal land management involves careful observation of ecological indicators: bird behavior that signals water, plant growth that indicates fire timing, animal movement that reveals seasonal change. This is empirical science embedded in spiritual practice. The Law requires asking permission of Country before taking resources — a practice that Western environmentalism is slowly recognizing as sustainable management. The 'Caring for Country' programs run by Aboriginal land councils across Australia formalize this ancient practice into contemporary land management. sections.SourceQuote:
— K. Langloh Parker, Australian Legendary Tales (1896) —

Having heard what the blacks wanted them to do, these famous climbers went to the yaraan tree and made a start at once. There was only light enough that first night for them to see to reach a fork in the tree about half-way up. There they camped, watched Mullyan away in the morning, and then climbed on. At last they reached the home of Mullyan. They watched their chance and then sneaked into his humpy.

When they were safely inside, they hastened to secrete a smouldering stick in one end of the humpy, taking care they were not seen by any of the women. Then they went quietly down again, no one the wiser of their coming or going. During the day the women heard sometimes a crackling noise, as of burning, but looking round they saw nothing, and as their own fire was safe, they took no notice, thinking it might have been caused by some grass having fallen into their fire.

After their descent from having hidden the smouldering fire stick, Bibbee and Murrawondah found the blacks and told them what they had done. Hearing that the plan was to burn out Mullyan, and fearing that the tree might fall, they all moved to some little distance, there to watch and wait for the end.

sections.Whisper: Aboriginal fire-stick farming (cultural burning) is now recognized as one of the most sophisticated land management systems ever developed. For at least 50,000 years, Aboriginal people burned small patches of Country in careful seasonal rotation, creating a mosaic of growth stages that maximized biodiversity, attracted game, promoted food plants, and prevented catastrophic wildfire. When colonization stopped these burns, fuel loads built up, leading to the devastating bushfires that now plague Australia. Victor Steffensen (Tagalaka nation) has led the revival of cultural burning through his book 'Fire Country' and the Firesticks Alliance. After the 2019-2020 Black Summer fires, Australian fire services increasingly collaborate with Aboriginal burning practitioners. This is 50,000-year-old science being rediscovered.

sections.Narration:

sections.SourceQuote:
— K. Langloh Parker, Australian Legendary Tales (1896) —

These, for the most part, are KINDER MARCHEN, though they include many aetiological myths, explanatory of the markings and habits of animals, the origin of constellations, and so forth. They are a savage edition of the METAMORPHOSES, and few unbiased students now doubt that the METAMORPHOSES are a very late and very artificial version of traditional tales as savage in origin as those of the Noongahburrah. I have read Mrs. Parker's collection with very great interest, with "human pleasure," merely for the story's sake. Children will find here the Jungle Book, never before printed, of black little boys and girls. The sympathy with, and knowledge of beast-life and bird-life are worthy of Mr. Kipling, and the grotesque names are just what children like. Dinewan and Goomblegubbon should take their place with Rikki Tikki and Mr. Kipling's other delightful creatures. But there is here no Mowgli, set apart in the jungle as a man. Man, bird, and beast are all blended in the Australian fancy as in that of Bushmen and Red Indians. All are of one kindred, all shade into each other; all obey the Bush Law as they obey the Jungle Law in Mr. Kipling's fascinating stories.

sections.Whisper: Aboriginal sharing protocols (sometimes called 'demand sharing' by anthropologists) are a sophisticated economic system that prevented wealth accumulation and ensured community survival for millennia. Kinship rules dictate who gets what portion of any food or resource — and the hunter or gatherer typically receives the LEAST desirable portion, ensuring generosity is structurally enforced. This is not charity but law. Refusal to share is one of the most serious social offenses. Western economics views sharing as voluntary generosity; Aboriginal Law views it as structural obligation — closer to taxation than charity, but embedded in kinship rather than government. See the work of David Graeber ('Debt: The First 5,000 Years') on how gift economies like Aboriginal systems challenge Western economic assumptions.

The Weeoombeens hurriedly discussed what plan they had better adopt to elude the black fellows, for well they knew that should they ever meet any of them again they would be killed without mercy. And as they talked they satisfied their hunger by eating some of the emu flesh.

After a while the black fellows returned, and soon was the stone removed from the entrance. Some of them crept into the hole, where, to their surprise, they found only the remains of the emu and no trace of the Weeoombeens. sections.Wonder: Do you have special ceremonies in your family — birthdays, holidays, bedtime rituals? What would happen if you stopped doing them?
sections.Whisper: Aboriginal ceremony is understood not as commemoration but as MAINTENANCE. The Dreaming is not a historical event that happened once — it is an ongoing process that requires human participation to continue. Ceremony re-sings the Songlines, renews the Dreaming, and maintains the health of Country. If ceremony stops, Country suffers — literally. This is not metaphor: when Aboriginal people were removed from Country and prevented from performing ceremony (as happened extensively under colonization), ecological degradation followed. The land needs its people. This reciprocal relationship — humans maintaining the world through ceremony — is found in many Indigenous traditions but is most explicitly articulated in Aboriginal Australian practice. Much ceremony is restricted and cannot be described here. What we can share: ceremony matters. See the Garma Festival (Yolngu, Arnhem Land) — the largest public Aboriginal cultural gathering in Australia.

— K. Langloh Parker, Australian Legendary Tales (1896) —

The boys took the boomerangs. They threw them one after another; but to their surprise not one of the boomerangs they threw touched the ground, but, instead, went whirling up out of sight. When they had finished throwing the boomerangs, all of which acted in the same way, whirling up through space, they prepared to start home again. But as they looked round they saw a huge whirlwind coming towards them. They were frightened and called out "Wurrawilberoo," for they knew there was a devil in the whirlwind. They laid hold of trees near at hand that it might not catch them. But the whirlwind spread out first one arm and rooted up one tree, then another arm, and rooted up another. The boys ran in fear from tree to tree, but each tree that they went to was torn up by the whirlwind. At last they ran to two mubboo or beef-wood trees, and clung tightly to them. After them rushed the whirlwind, sweeping all before it, and when it reached the mubboo trees, to which the boys were clinging, it tore them from their roots and bore them upward swiftly, giving the boys no time to leave go, so they were borne upward clinging to the mubboo trees.

sections.Wonder: When someone you love goes away — even to school or on a trip — it's okay to feel sad. What helps you feel better when you miss someone?
sections.Whisper: Sorry Business is the Aboriginal term for mourning practices, which are among the most structured grief protocols in any human culture. Practices vary by nation but commonly include: extended mourning periods where the community shares the burden of grief, avoidance of the deceased person's name (sometimes for years — which is why media now include warnings before showing images of deceased Aboriginal people), smoking ceremonies to cleanse the deceased's dwelling, and sometimes physical expressions of grief. The depth and community nature of Sorry Business contrasts sharply with Western grief practices, where mourners are often expected to 'move on' quickly. Aboriginal psychologists increasingly advocate for Sorry Business-informed grief counseling. See the work of Professor Pat Dudgeon (Bardi) on Aboriginal social and emotional wellbeing.

sections.SourceQuote:
— K. Langloh Parker, Australian Legendary Tales (1896) —

The manners and rites of the natives were far the most archaic of all with which we are acquainted. Temples they had none: no images of gods, no altars of sacrifice; scarce any memorials of the dead. Their worship at best was offered in hymns to some vague, half-forgotten deity or First Maker of things, a god decrepit from age or all but careless of his children. Spirits were known and feared, but scarcely defined or described. Sympathetic magic, and perhaps a little hypnotism, were all their science. Kings and nations they knew not; they were wanderers, houseless and homeless. Custom was king; yet custom was tenacious, irresistible, and as complex in minute details as the etiquette of Spanish kings, or the ritual of the Flamens of Rome. The archaic intricacies and taboos of the customs and regulations of marriage might puzzle a mathematician, and may, when unravelled, explain the less complicated prohibitions of a totemism less antique. The people themselves in their struggle for existence had developed great ingenuities.

sections.Wonder: The Dreaming isn't over — it's happening right now, even as you fall asleep. What do you think the Dreaming is dreaming about tonight?
sections.Whisper: The final and most important teaching: the Dreaming is NOW. It is not a creation myth in the past tense. It is the eternal present from which all time unfolds. Aboriginal children are understood to be Dreaming beings — each child's spirit comes from a specific place in Country, and their birth is the Dreaming continuing to create. This means that Aboriginal culture is not 'ancient' in the Western sense of 'old and finished' — it is ancient in the sense of 'always happening.' When Aboriginal activists fight for land rights, they are not preserving a museum piece. They are maintaining a living, ongoing creation. The Dreaming continues through every Aboriginal person who walks Country, sings the songs, and passes the stories on. See the Uluru Statement from the Heart (2017) — a Dreaming document for the 21st century, inviting all Australians into relationship with the oldest continuous culture on Earth.

metadata.tradition: Pan-Aboriginal understanding of the Dreaming as eternal present
